CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) with the main contributions stated early and a clear description of the joint CNN + TransE-style loss and rating constraint. Notation and objectives are described (final weighted loss) and hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility, and related work coverage is thorough. Some presentation details are only summarized here (figures/tables effectiveness not fully described) and writing is described as adequate rather than polished, so minor clarity improvements could be made. | B(4): Overall the paper appears well organized and logically structured (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) with the main contributions stated early (joint CNN + TransE-style behavioral embeddings for cold-start). Notation and methodological descriptions (CNN text embeddings, TransE-like margin loss, rating-constraint loss, combined objective) are reported consistently and hyperparameters are provided. Figures/tables and examples are described as effective enough to support results on the Yelp cold-start evaluation, and related work coverage is thorough. Writing quality is adequate, though a few sections could be tightened to remove minor ambiguity and improve flow for readers focused on deployment/robustness details.

Writing quality is adequate, though a few sections could be tightened to remove minor ambiguity and improve flow for readers focused on deployment/robustness details.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an NLP/ML problem (review spam detection) using neural text encoders (CNN) combined with graph-style behavioral embeddings (TransE-like loss), evaluated on standard Yelp datasets with appropriate metrics—this sits squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It targets the right audience by combining representation learning and applied spam detection and compares against suitable baselines. My only minor concern is that the contribution is somewhat application-focused (engineering a joint embedding for cold-start) rather than a clear foundational advance in modeling, which may make it borderline for the very top conferences unless the empirical gains and analysis are substantial and reproducible. | B(4): This paper addresses a clearly relevant NLP/ML problem—review spam detection with a cold-start focus—and introduces a joint text+behavior embedding model that builds on familiar neural (CNN) and graph-embedding (TransE-like) techniques, which fits well with top conference themes. Evaluation uses standard datasets (Yelp), sensible baselines (linguistic and behavioral features, independent CNN) and common metrics (Precision/Recall/F1/Accuracy), meeting venue expectations. Minor scope concerns arise because the contribution is applied and security-focused (deployment/robustness aspects and threat modeling are not emphasized in the summary), so it is slightly more specialized than some theoretical ML work but still a good fit for NLP/ML venues.

It targets the right audience by combining representation learning and applied spam detection and compares against suitable baselines. SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claim—that jointly learning CNN-based text embeddings with TransE-style behavioral embeddings improves cold-start spam detection—is supported by comparative experiments on the Yelp dataset against reasonable baselines (linguistic SVM, behavioral SVM, independent CNN) and reported metrics (precision/recall/F1/accuracy). However, from the summary I note missing details that affect reproducibility and statistical confidence: code is not mentioned, there is no indication of multiple runs, random seeds, or significance testing, and formal derivations of the TransE-like losses are not described here. Baselines and ablations are appropriate and the conclusions appear consistent with the reported results, but the lack of explicit statistical rigor and released implementation are minor concerns that prevent a top score. | B(4): The paper's main claim—that a joint CNN+TransE-style behavioral embedding improves cold-start review spam detection—is supported by experimental improvements in F1 and accuracy on the Yelp datasets and by comparisons to reasonable baselines (independent CNN, linguistic and behavioral-feature SVMs). The mathematical formulation appears standard and complete for the learning objectives (no formal proofs were required), but the summary does not report statistical significance testing or broader robustness analyses (e.g., alternative cold-start simulations or adversarial behavior), which are important for validating generality. Overall the methodology is sound and results justify the conclusions, with minor concerns about evaluation realism and missing significance/robustness checks.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claim—that jointly learning CNN-based text embeddings with TransE-style behavioral embeddings improves cold-start spam detection—is supported by comparative experiments on the Yelp dataset against reasonable baselines (linguistic SVM, behavioral SVM, independent CNN) and reported metrics (precision/recall/F1/accuracy). However, from the summary I note missing details that affect reproducibility and statistical confidence: code is not mentioned, there is no indication of multiple runs, random seeds, or significance testing, and formal derivations of the TransE-like losses are not described here. Baselines and ablations are appropriate and the conclusions appear consistent with the reported results, but the lack of explicit statistical rigor and released implementation are minor concerns that prevent a top score.

Overall the methodology is sound and results justify the conclusions, with minor concerns about evaluation realism and missing significance/robustness checks.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against sensible and task-relevant baselines (linguistic SVMs, traditional behavioral-feature SVMs, and an independent supervised CNN) on the same Yelp cold-start split and uses standard metrics (precision/recall/F1/accuracy), which supports fair evaluation. It also situates itself relative to prior behavioral work (e.g., Mukherjee et al., Rayana & Akoglu and other opinion-spammer studies). However, the comparison omits some stronger contemporary neural/graph-based baselines and lacks reported multiple runs/statistical tests to quantify variability, so while comparisons are good they are not fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper compares its joint embedding model to sensible baselines: traditional behavioral feature SVMs (RL, RD, MCS), linguistic bigram SVMs, and an ablated independent CNN, all evaluated on the same Yelp datasets used in Mukherjee et al. and Rayana & Akoglu. Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and the authors analyze why behavioral features fail in cold-start and how the joint model helps, but the evaluation omits discussion of some other modern joint/textual or graph‑embedding approaches that could be relevant and there is limited evidence they acknowledge any prior methods that perform comparably. Overall the empirical comparison is strong but not fully comprehensive with respect to the wider recent literature.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against sensible and task-relevant baselines (linguistic SVMs, traditional behavioral-feature SVMs, and an independent supervised CNN) on the same Yelp cold-start split and uses standard metrics (precision/recall/F1/accuracy), which supports fair evaluation. It also situates itself relative to prior behavioral work (e.g., Mukherjee et al., Rayana & Akoglu and other opinion-spammer studies). However, the comparison omits some stronger contemporary neural/graph-based baselines and lacks reported multiple runs/statistical tests to quantify variability, so while comparisons are good they are not fully comprehensive.

Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and the authors analyze why behavioral features fail in cold-start and how the joint model helps, but the evaluation omits discussion of some other modern joint/textual or graph‑embedding approaches that could be relevant and there is limited evidence they acknowledge any prior methods that perform comparably. Overall the empirical comparison is strong but not fully comprehensive with respect to the wider recent literature.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper combines known components (CNN text encoders, TransE-style graph embeddings, and margin/rating-constraint losses) into a joint model for the cold-start review-spam problem, which is a reasonable and practically useful synthesis but not a fundamentally new paradigm. Prior work (e.g., Mukherjee et al., Rayana & Akoglu, and earlier behavioral-footprint studies) already leverages behavioral signals for spam detection; this paper’s novelty lies mainly in jointly learning textual and behavioral embeddings for cold-start scenarios and demonstrating empirical gains. From a reproducibility and research-novelty perspective this is an incremental yet non-trivial extension that would inform practitioners but is unlikely to surprise experts as a radically new idea. | B(4): The paper presents a notable and reasonably novel combination: jointly learning CNN-based text embeddings with TransE-like behavioral/reviewer/product embeddings to address the cold-start review spam problem, which differentiates it from prior behavioral-footprint work (e.g., Mukherjee et al., Rayana & Akoglu) that used hand-crafted features. Using a margin loss to encode global graph behavior and integrating rating constraints alongside text is a non-trivial extension beyond separately applied CNNs or behavioral SVMs and would inform practitioners working on sparse-behavior scenarios. However, the core components (CNN for text, TransE-style graph embeddings, and standard behavioral features) are established techniques, so the contribution is more an impactful integration and application than a fundamentally new method.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper combines known components (CNN text encoders, TransE-style graph embeddings, and margin/rating-constraint losses) into a joint model for the cold-start review-spam problem, which is a reasonable and practically useful synthesis but not a fundamentally new paradigm. Prior work (e.g., Mukherjee et al., Rayana & Akoglu, and earlier behavioral-footprint studies) already leverages behavioral signals for spam detection; this paper’s novelty lies mainly in jointly learning textual and behavioral embeddings for cold-start scenarios and demonstrating empirical gains. From a reproducibility and research-novelty perspective this is an incremental yet non-trivial extension that would inform practitioners but is unlikely to surprise experts as a radically new idea.

Using a margin loss to encode global graph behavior and integrating rating constraints alongside text is a non-trivial extension beyond separately applied CNNs or behavioral SVMs and would inform practitioners working on sparse-behavior scenarios. However, the core components (CNN for text, TransE-style graph embeddings, and standard behavioral features) are established techniques, so the contribution is more an impactful integration and application than a fundamentally new method.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper provides a solid empirical evaluation on the Yelp cold-start setting, compares the proposed joint CNN+TransE-style model against reasonable baselines (linguistic and behavioral SVMs, an independent CNN), and reports hyperparameters and domain-specific observations (e.g., smaller gains in restaurants). Related work appears well covered (including Mukherjee et al. and Rayana & Akoglu), and limitations of behavioral features in cold-start are discussed. However, crucial reproducibility and robustness details are missing or unclear in the summary: I see no mention of multiple randomized runs, confidence intervals or statistical tests, no explicit ablation/sensitivity study of the joint loss components (e.g., the rating-constraint or loss-weight choices), and no code release—leaving some uncertainty about how much of the gain is attributable to specific design choices. | B(4): The experiments compare the joint model against reasonable baselines (linguistic SVM, traditional behavioral features, and an independent CNN) on a real Yelp cold-start setup and report standard metrics, with hyperparameters provided — supporting the main claims. Ablation is present in the form of the independent CNN vs joint model, but the paper lacks broader sensitivity analyses (e.g., robustness to adversarial behavior, cross-dataset or cross-domain evaluation, and runtime/scalability studies). Related work coverage is described as thorough, and the paper acknowledges limitations (e.g., dependence on availability of behavioral traces), but code is not mentioned which hinders exact reproducibility. Overall the empirical evaluation is solid with minor gaps that prevent it from being extremely comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a solid empirical evaluation on the Yelp cold-start setting, compares the proposed joint CNN+TransE-style model against reasonable baselines (linguistic and behavioral SVMs, an independent CNN), and reports hyperparameters and domain-specific observations (e.g., smaller gains in restaurants). Related work appears well covered (including Mukherjee et al. and Rayana & Akoglu), and limitations of behavioral features in cold-start are discussed. However, crucial reproducibility and robustness details are missing or unclear in the summary: I see no mention of multiple randomized runs, confidence intervals or statistical tests, no explicit ablation/sensitivity study of the joint loss components (e.g., the rating-constraint or loss-weight choices), and no code release—leaving some uncertainty about how much of the gain is attributable to specific design choices.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments compare the joint model against reasonable baselines (linguistic SVM, traditional behavioral features, and an independent CNN) on a real Yelp cold-start setup and report standard metrics, with hyperparameters provided — supporting the main claims. Ablation is present in the form of the independent CNN vs joint model, but the paper lacks broader sensitivity analyses (e.g., robustness to adversarial behavior, cross-dataset or cross-domain evaluation, and runtime/scalability studies). Related work coverage is described as thorough, and the paper acknowledges limitations (e.g., dependence on availability of behavioral traces), but code is not mentioned which hinders exact reproducibility. Overall the empirical evaluation is solid with minor gaps that prevent it from being extremely comprehensive.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper targets an important practical problem—cold-start review spam detection—and proposes a joint text+behavior embedding (CNN for text + TransE-style behavioral embeddings with a rating constraint) that empirically improves F1/accuracy on a standard Yelp cold-start evaluation. While the approach is practically useful and likely to influence follow-up work that combines graph-style behavioral signals with neural text encoders, the contribution appears incremental (novel combination rather than a paradigm shift) and its gains depend on availability of behavioral data and domain characteristics (authors note smaller gains in restaurants). Overall, this is a moderate-impact contribution that practitioners and researchers in spam detection and related fraud/abuse tasks will likely build on, but it is unlikely to fundamentally change the field. | B(3): The paper tackles an important and practical problem—cold-start review spam detection—by proposing a joint text+behavior embedding model and shows empirical gains on a real Yelp benchmark versus linguistic and behavioral baselines. These results are useful to researchers and practitioners working on early detection when behavioral signals are sparse, and the TransE-style graph encoding combined with CNN text embeddings is a plausible, reusable approach. However, the impact appears moderate: improvements are demonstrated on a single dataset/domain and the contribution is an application of existing embedding ideas rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, so it is unlikely to be transformative across a broad range of adversarial detection tasks.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper targets an important practical problem—cold-start review spam detection—and proposes a joint text+behavior embedding (CNN for text + TransE-style behavioral embeddings with a rating constraint) that empirically improves F1/accuracy on a standard Yelp cold-start evaluation. While the approach is practically useful and likely to influence follow-up work that combines graph-style behavioral signals with neural text encoders, the contribution appears incremental (novel combination rather than a paradigm shift) and its gains depend on availability of behavioral data and domain characteristics (authors note smaller gains in restaurants). Overall, this is a moderate-impact contribution that practitioners and researchers in spam detection and related fraud/abuse tasks will likely build on, but it is unlikely to fundamentally change the field.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important and practical problem—cold-start review spam detection—by proposing a joint text+behavior embedding model and shows empirical gains on a real Yelp benchmark versus linguistic and behavioral baselines. These results are useful to researchers and practitioners working on early detection when behavioral signals are sparse, and the TransE-style graph encoding combined with CNN text embeddings is a plausible, reusable approach. However, the impact appears moderate: improvements are demonstrated on a single dataset/domain and the contribution is an application of existing embedding ideas rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, so it is unlikely to be transformative across a broad range of adversarial detection tasks.

main_claims: Proposes a novel neural network to address the cold-start problem in review spam detection by jointly embedding textual and behavioral information.
Encodes global behavioral information from the review graph using a TransE-like embedding and jointly learns review text embeddings (via CNN) and rating embeddings to improve detection.
Demonstrates the joint model outperforms an independent supervised CNN and traditional linguistic/behavioral feature based methods on a Yelp cold-start evaluation, improving F1 and accuracy.
_summary.methodology: Jointly learn review text embeddings with a CNN and behavioral/product/reviewer embeddings using a TransE-style margin loss, plus a rating-constraint loss; final loss is a weighted combination of the two objectives.
datasets: Yelp dataset (as used in Mukherjee et al. 2013b,c; Rayana and Akoglu 2015)
baselines: Word bigrams (linguistic features) used with SVM
Traditional behavioral features (RL, RD, MCS) used with SVM
Independent supervised CNN (same CNN part without graph/behavioral info)
SVM classifiers combining LF and BF variants
limitations: Traditional behavioral features are ineffective in the cold-start setting due to poor behavioral information and can increase false positives (lower F1 and recall).
Behavioral-feature improvements depend on availability of additional user behavior (noted smaller improvement in restaurant domain due to fewer subsequent reviews).

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer and researcher with extensive experience developing and evaluating neural architectures for text classification and graph-based representation learning in industry and academia. Has led reproducibility audits and implemented production-grade spam detection systems.
traits: insists on clear experimental protocol details (data splits, hyperparameters, random seeds, compute resources)
looks for strong, well-implemented baselines and ablation studies to attribute gains
evaluates statistical rigor (multiple runs, confidence intervals, significance tests)
checks for implementability and clarity of loss functions, training schedules, and model capacity
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the experiments and methods are described and executed in a way that supports the claimed improvements and allows reproducibility; requests additional experiments or clarifications when necessary.

reviewer_b.name: Applied NLP / Security Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in NLP and adversarial information quality with a track record of work on review spam, fake news, and user behavior modeling; experienced in deploying detection methods and assessing real-world impact.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior work on behavioral/textual joint models and cold-start in spam detection
values practical impact, robustness to adversarial behavior, and real-world deployment constraints
notes limitations in evaluation realism (dataset representativeness, threat models) and potential ethical concerns
appreciates clarity about where the method advances the field and how it could be adopted or evaded
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper's significance and novelty in the context of prior work and practical applicability, highlighting potential impact, limitations, and directions for follow-up research.
